title: Sweat on Cotton
stainChemistry: Classification: Protein. Sweat stains have three components: proteins (albumin, immunoglobulins), lipids (sebum from skin), and minerals (sodium chloride, urea). The yellow discoloration on white fabric is caused by the reaction between aluminum (from antiperspirant) and sweat proteins — aluminum-protein complexes that bond to cellulose. On cotton (cellulose fiber): protein stains bond to fibers via peptide-to-fiber hydrogen bonds and hydrophobic interactions. Heat denatures the protein, cross-linking it to the substrate. Treatment: cold water flush to prevent heat-setting, followed by alkaline protease enzyme digestion to hydrolyze peptide bonds.
whyThisWorks: Sweat contains proteins and salts that build up and bond over time. Cotton absorbs liquids quickly and holds stains deep in the weave. Cold water prevents the protein from setting permanently, and enzyme-based cleaners break it down so it rinses clean.
defaultAssumption: Treat as unknown age / possibly dried.

steps: 1. Apply Protein Formula to the stain and tamp gently with a spotting brush. This digestant breaks down blood, milk, egg, and body fluid proteins. Work from the edge toward the center. On wool or silk, use reduced dwell time (3 minutes) to protect the fiber. Dwell 5–10 minutes.
2. Apply NSD and work gently. This neutral detergent removes the digested protein particles and any residual spotting agent. Flush with steam gun or cool water spray. Dwell 3–5 minutes.
3. Apply diluted acetic acid (1:10) to neutralize any remaining alkaline residue and restore the fabric's natural state. Blot dry with clean absorbent cloth. Dwell 1–2 minutes.
professionalProtocol.temperature: cold — hot water sets protein stains permanently
warnings: Using hot water on this stain — sets it permanently
Rubbing instead of blotting — spreads the stain
Not testing in an inconspicuous area first
Using too aggressive a chemical without checking surface compatibility

steps: 1. Rinse with cold water right away. Blot with a clean white cloth — don't rub.
2. Mix a small squirt of dish soap into cold water. Blot the solution onto the stain.
3. Let it sit for 5–10 minutes. Don't use any heat.
4. Blot with a clean white cloth until the stain fades.
5. Rinse with cold water and air dry — don't use a dryer until the stain is gone.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Try an enzyme presoak (Nature's Miracle or OxiClean Versatile) — apply and let it soak for 30–60 minutes. If the stain survives two rounds, a professional cleaner can use concentrated agents.
warnings: Always blot — never rub. Rubbing pushes the stain deeper into the fabric.
Never use hot water on a protein stain — heat permanently sets it.
Never use the dryer until the stain is completely gone.

neverDo: Use hot water — sets protein stain permanently
Apply heat before stain is fully treated
Use alcohol-based products — alcohol permanently sets protein stains beyond professional recovery
fiberSensitivities: Cotton tolerates most agents including bleach on whites. For colored cotton, test peroxide first. High heat can set protein stains. OxiClean safe on cotton.

escalation.when: Sweat stain persists after full protocol — particularly aluminum-protein pit stains
escalation.whatToTell: Sweat stain on cotton, protein and mineral components. Aluminum-protein complex if antiperspirant involved. Treated at home with dish soap and cold water. Please use enzyme-based protocol.

contextQuestions: Is this a fresh stain or long-term pit stain buildup?
Was antiperspirant (aluminum) or just deodorant (no aluminum) involved?
Is the fabric white (showing yellow) or colored?
Has the garment been through the dryer with the stain present?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Long-term pit stains with antiperspirant are much harder to remove than fresh sweat stains. The yellow aluminum-protein complex requires a different approach — white vinegar or citric acid can help break down the aluminum component before enzyme treatment. Dryer exposure sets the stain significantly.
